sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=34, col=13), RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 29, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left green absorbed the left dark outline by moving into its interior! The dark outline disappeared. Level not complete - need to do the same with the right green and right dark outline.
- Plan: Swap to give right green W center, then navigate it to the right dark outline at (26-30, 44-48).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 28 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After swap and RIGHT:
- Left green: moved from (33-35, 12-14) to (33-35, 15-17), center=W
- Right green: stayed at (30-32, 27-29), center=c
Wait, the LEFT green moved RIGHT again! It was at cols 12-14 and moved to 15-17. So the RIGHT action affected the left green (which has W center), not the right green (which has c center).
This confirms: only the green square with W center moves, and it moves in the direction of the arrow key. The green square with c center doesn't move.
So the problem is: I need the RIGHT green to have W center so it can move to the right dark outline. But when I swap, the LEFT green gets W center and moves instead.
Wait, but I DID swap: after the swap, left green got W center and right green got c center. Then RIGHT moved the left green (with W center) to the right.
So the issue is: the left green keeps getting W center and moving. I need to get the right green to have W center AND be able to move to the right dark outline.
But the right dark outline is at cols 44-48, which is far to the right of the corridor. The right green is at cols 27-29 in the corridor. Even if it has W center, it can only move to about col 32 (the white block) before hitting the purple bar.
Unless... the right green can move UP/DOWN too? If it can move UP, it could go to rows 26-28, which aligns with the right dark outline's rows 26-30. But it still can't reach cols 44-48.
Hmm, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal isn't to absorb both dark outlines. Maybe there's a different mechanic I'm missing.
Let me think about what happened:
1. Left green moved into left dark outline → dark outline disappeared
2. Level not complete
What if the goal is different? What if absorbing the dark outline is NOT the goal, but rather a side effect? Or what if I need to do something else after absorbing one?
Actually, wait. Let me look at the current state more carefully. After the swap, the left green has W center and the right green has c center. When I pressed RIGHT, the left green moved RIGHT (from 12-14 to 15-17). This undid the absorption of the left dark outline!
Let me check if the left dark outline reappeared... Looking at the segmentation output:
- w px=9 bound=[[33, 12], [33, 14], [35, 14], [35, 12]] - this is the left dark outline's interior!
So the left dark outline DID reappear when the left green moved out of it!
This means:
1. Moving the green into the dark outline "absorbs" it temporarily
2. Moving the green out "releases" it
So the goal might be to have BOTH green squares inside their respective dark outlines simultaneously. But the right green can't reach the right dark outline because it's blocked by the purple bar.
Unless... I need to think about this differently. What if the purple bar is not a barrier but a portal or transporter? What if moving the right green RIGHT when it's adjacent to the purple bar teleports it to the other side?
Or what if I need to move the right green UP first, then RIGHT?
Let me try: first get the right green to have W center, then try moving it UP to see if it can move vertically.
Actually wait, I need to first swap back so the right green has W center. Currently left green has W center. Let me swap.
But I'm running low on actions (28 of 28). Let me be strategic.
Let me try: swap (right gets W), then UP to see if right green can move vertically.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT action moved the LEFT green (which has W center), not the right green (c center). The left dark outline reappeared when left green moved out. So absorption is temporary - green must STAY inside to keep it absorbed. Need BOTH greens inside their dark outlines simultaneously.
Plan: Swap to give right green W center, try moving it UP to see if it can reach the right dark outline's row range.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left green W at (33-35, 15-17), right green c at (30-32, 27-29)
# Need to swap so right green gets W
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 16}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After swap:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")

# Try UP to see if right green can move vertically
result2 = action(['UP'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter UP:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result2.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
